quality.rationale: 标准高维球面坐标参数化与微分代换验证内蕴度规，逻辑链条完整但属纯几何推导

origin.question: Problem 6.2. Show that the line element$$\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathsf {R}^{2} [ \mathrm {d} a^{2} + \sin^{2} \alpha (\mathrm {d} \dot {\theta}^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^{2}) ]$$represents a hypersphere of radius $\mathbf{R}$ in Euclidean 4-space, i.e. a locus of points a distance $\mathbf{R}$ from a given point.## SolutionSolution 6.2. The metric in Euclidean 4-space is$$\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {d x}_{1}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}_{2}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}_{3}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}_{4}^{2}. \tag {1}$$The equation of a hypersphere of radius $\mathbb{R}$ is$$\mathbf {x}_{1}^{2} + \mathbf {x}_{2}^{2} + \mathbf {x}_{3}^{2} + \mathbf {x}_{4}^{2} = R^{2}. \tag {2}$$By analogy with the 3-dimensional case, introduce coordinates on the hypersphere:$$\begin{array}{l}\mathbf {x}_{4} = \text sf {R c o s} \alpha \\\mathbf {x}_{3} = \mathbf {R} \sin a \cos \theta \tag {3} \\\mathbf {x}_{2} = \mathrm {R} \sin a \sin \theta \cos \phi \\\mathbf {x}_{1} = \mathrm {R} \sin \alpha \sin \theta \sin \phi . \\\end{array}$$Then Equation (2) is automatically satisfied; taking the differential of Equation (3) with $R$ constant, and substituting in Equation (1) yields the required metric of the hypersphere.
origin.solution: The metric in Euclidean 4-space is$$\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {d x}_{1}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}_{2}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}_{3}^{2} + \mathrm {d x}_{4}^{2}. \tag {1}$$The equation of a hypersphere of radius $\mathbb{R}$ is$$\mathbf {x}_{1}^{2} + \mathbf {x}_{2}^{2} + \mathbf {x}_{3}^{2} + \mathbf {x}_{4}^{2} = R^{2}. \tag {2}$$By analogy with the 3-dimensional case, introduce coordinates on the hypersphere:$$\begin{array}{l}\mathbf {x}_{4} = \text sf {R c o s} \alpha \\\mathbf {x}_{3} = \mathbf {R} \sin a \cos \theta \tag {3} \\\mathbf {x}_{2} = \mathrm {R} \sin a \sin \theta \cos \phi \\\mathbf {x}_{1} = \mathrm {R} \sin \alpha \sin \theta \sin \phi . \\\end{array}$$Then Equation (2) is automatically satisfied; taking the differential of Equation (3) with $R$ constant, and substituting in Equation (1) yields the required metric of the hypersphere.
